auto_merge.commit_message:
Another thing raised by @devdanzin's automated analysis...

There's quite a few places where we discard all errors with PyErr_Clear.
That's usually close to what's intended, but it also discards
"BaseException" which are typically things that we don't want to ignore
like MemoryError, KeyboardInterrupt.

I've usually adopted the pattern or "discard regular exception; keep
base exception". One exception is `__Pyx_PyDict_GetItemStr`
- this minics the interface for `PyDict_GetItem` so in that case I've
just followed their lead and done "write unraisable".

repo.description: The most widely used Python to C compiler
repo.name: cython

body:
Another thing raised by @devdanzin's automated analysis...

There's quite a few places where we discard all errors with PyErr_Clear. That's usually close to what's intended, but it also discards "BaseException" which are typically things that we don't want to ignore like MemoryError, KeyboardInterrupt.

I've usually adopted the pattern or "discard regular exception; keep base exception". One exception is `__Pyx_PyDict_GetItemStr`
- this minics the interface for `PyDict_GetItem` so in that case I've just followed their lead and done "write unraisable".

There's a few examples which I haven't addressed here because they looked like they needed a bit more thought:

* MergeVTables
* __Pyx_setup_reduce and __Pyx_setup_reduce_is_named (I have a branch for this https://github.com/da-woods/cython/tree/setup-reduce but it depends on this PR)
* `__Pyx_Py_UNICODE_is_*`
* `__Pyx_PyType_GetFullyQualifiedName` (in this case it should probably be failable, and there's a better option in recent Python versions).

comment_data.body:
`PyErr_WriteUnraisable()` clears the exception after printing it.

```suggestion
```

This seems worth moving into a utility function, like `if (!__Pyx_ClearException()) return NULL;`.

Or maybe `if (!__Pyx_IgnoreException(PyExc_Exception)) return NULL;` to cover more use cases. A bit more verbose but might be more explicit in what it does.
I  did a slight variation with an optional first argument for the exception. Just because sometimes you can avoid looking up the exception state twice.

Hmm. It seems annoying to have an opaque `NULL` value as a required argument here. Could you add a macro that hides it? Python adds `Given` (`PyErr_GivenExceptionMatches`) when an exception reference is provided. It seems that the normal case is that we don't have a reference to an exception, so I'd reserve `__Pyx_IgnoreException` for that.

commit.message:
Try to avoid unguarded PyErr_Clear

Another thing raised by @devdanzin's automated analysis...

There's quite a few places where we discard all errors with
PyErr_Clear. That's usually close to what's intended, but
it also discards "BaseException" which are typically things that
we don't want to ignore like MemoryError, KeyboardInterrupt.

I've usually adopted the pattern or "discard regular exception;
keep base exception". One exception is `__Pyx_PyDict_GetItemStr`
- this minics the interface for `PyDict_GetItem` so in that case
I've just followed their lead and done "write unraisable".

There's a few examples which I haven't addressed here because
they looked like they needed a bit more thought:

* MergeVTables
* __Pyx_setup_reduce and __Pyx_setup_reduce_is_named
* `__Pyx_Py_UNICODE_is_*`
* `__Pyx_PyType_GetFullyQualifiedName` (in this case it should probably
  be failable, and there's a better option in recent Python versions).
Check if error occurred before FormatUnraisable

title: Try to avoid unguarded PyErr_Clear
